1:
"And when Rachel saw that she bare Jacob no children, Rachel envied her sister; and said unto Jacob, Give me children, or else I die." The witnesses note Rachel's envy and impatient cry — the crux of her discontent. On "Rachel saw that she bare Jacob no children, Rachel envied her sister," the grammatical voices note the envy. Ellicott and Gill note that "Rachel saw that she bare Jacob no children, Rachel envied her sister" — Rachel, barren while Leah bore four sons (29:31-35), envied her sister; the sin of envy and discontent; the beloved wife (29:30) grieved at her barrenness; the rivalry of the sisters breaking out.

On "Give me children, or else I die," interpreters expound the cry. The puritan Henry and the dominant reading note that Rachel said to Jacob, "Give me children, or else I die" — an impatient, fretful, immoderate demand: her heart set inordinately on children (one comfort lacking embittered all the rest); the discontent that could not be satisfied; the impatience that, as it were, would die without children; Henry notes, "we are very apt to be discontented with what God has appointed us"; the fretting of the envious heart, demanding of Jacob what only God could give (v.2); the sinful, immoderate craving (contrast Hannah's prayer, 1 Samuel 1:10-11, who took her grief to God).

On the demand, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the misdirection: Rachel demanded children of Jacob ("Give me children"), as if her husband could give what God withholds; the misdirected craving (Jacob rightly answers, Am I in God's stead? v.2); the discontent that looked to man, not God; the lesson that children are the gift of God (Psalm 127:3), to be sought of him. The verse displays Rachel's envy and impatient cry.

The witnesses agree the substance: Rachel, envying Leah, cried to Jacob, Give me children, or else I die (Ellicott, Henry, Calvin, Gill). The cry bears its weight as the crux of her discontent: the envy of her sister and the impatient, immoderate demand — her heart set inordinately on children, fretting, as if she would die without them; the discontent that demanded of Jacob what only God could give (v.2; Psalm 127:3); the misdirected craving (contrast Hannah, 1 Samuel 1). The verse displays the envious Rachel, rebuked by Jacob (v.2).
2:
"And Jacob's anger was kindled against Rachel: and he said, Am I in God's stead, who hath withheld from thee the fruit of the womb?" The witnesses note Jacob's reply that children are the gift of God. On "Jacob's anger was kindled against Rachel," the grammatical voices note the displeasure. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Jacob's anger was kindled against Rachel" — Jacob was displeased at Rachel's fretful, misdirected demand (v.1); the just (if not wholly patient) displeasure at her impatience; the husband grieved that she demanded of him what was God's to give; the rebuke of her discontent.

On "Am I in God's stead, who hath withheld from thee the fruit of the womb?" interpreters expound the reply. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note Jacob's answer — "Am I in God's stead, who hath withheld from thee the fruit of the womb?": Jacob disclaimed the power to give children (he is not God); it is God, not man, who opens and shuts the womb (29:31; 1 Samuel 1:5); God had withheld children from Rachel; the children are God's gift (Psalm 127:3), to be sought of him, not demanded of the husband; Jacob's reply, though sharp, rightly directed Rachel to God; the acknowledgment that fruitfulness is in God's hand alone.

On "who hath withheld from thee the fruit of the womb," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the providence: God had withheld the fruit of the womb from Rachel; the sovereign hand of God in giving and withholding children; the lesson for Rachel (and all) to seek of God, in prayer (as she at last did, v.22, God remembered Rachel), what God alone can give; the gift of children in God's hand. The verse displays Jacob's reply that children are the gift of God.

The verse displays the answering Jacob, given Bilhah by Rachel (v.3).

14:

The witnesses note the mandrakes and Rachel's request — the crux of the dudaim. On "Reuben... found mandrakes in the field, and brought them unto his mother Leah," the grammatical voices note the mandrakes. Ellicott and Gill note that "Reuben... found mandrakes (Hebrew dudaim) in the field" — Reuben (Leah's eldest, a child) found mandrakes at wheat harvest and brought them to Leah; the dudaim ("love-apples") variously identified: the mandrake (a plant whose fruit and root were anciently reputed to promote love and fertility), or some other fruit/flower (jasmine, citron, or fragrant blossoms — the witnesses note the identification is uncertain); the plants prized (by some) for their supposed power to aid conception.

On the mandrakes and conception, interpreters weigh the crux. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the supposed virtue of the dudaim (to promote fertility); yet the witnesses are careful: the mandrakes did not in fact give the children — for it was Leah (not Rachel) who, after the bargain, conceived (vv.17-18), while Rachel, who got the mandrakes, remained barren until "God remembered Rachel" (v.22); thus the narrative pointedly shows that fruitfulness was of God, not of the mandrakes (the supposed remedy failing where God had not given, succeeding only where God gave); the witnesses warn against the superstition that ascribed conception to the plant, when it was God's gift (v.22; Psalm 127:3).

On "Give me, I pray thee, of thy son's mandrakes," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note Rachel's request: Rachel, still barren, desired the mandrakes (hoping, it seems, they might help her conceive — the very superstition the story refutes); the request that led to the bargain (v.15) and Leah's bearing (vv.16-18); the irony that Rachel got the mandrakes but not (thereby) the child. The verse displays the mandrakes and Rachel's request.

The witnesses agree the substance: Reuben found mandrakes, and Rachel asked Leah for them (Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The dudaim bear a crux: the "mandrakes" (love-apples), variously identified, anciently reputed to promote fertility — yet the narrative refutes the superstition, for Rachel, who got them, stayed barren till God remembered her (v.22), while Leah (without them) conceived (vv.17-18); fruitfulness is of God, not the plant (Psalm 127:3). 15:

On "thou hast taken my husband," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the grievance. Calvin and Gill note Leah's grievance — that Rachel had the chief place in Jacob's affection (29:30); the sad fruit of the rivalry; the unloved wife's bitterness; the contest for the husband.

On "he shall lie with thee to night for thy son's mandrakes," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the bargain: Rachel bargained — Leah might have Jacob that night in exchange for the mandrakes; the sad spectacle of the sisters bargaining over their husband; the rivalry reduced to a trade; the bargain that (by God's overruling) led to Leah's conceiving Issachar (vv.16-18); the providence working even through the unseemly contest. The verse displays the bargain over the mandrakes and the husband.

16:

17:

18:

22:
"And God remembered Rachel, and God hearkened to her, and opened her womb." The witnesses note God remembering Rachel and opening her womb — the crux of God's remembering. On "God remembered Rachel," interpreters expound the remembering. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and the dominant reading note that "God remembered Rachel" — not that God had forgotten her (he forgets none of his), but the anthropomorphism marks the effect produced: God now acted for Rachel, regarded her, and gave what he had long withheld; "the memory of God is named from the effect produced"; the turning-point — after all the rivalry, the handmaids, and the mandrakes (which gave nothing, v.14), God himself, in his own time, remembered the barren Rachel and gave her a child; the gift wholly of God.

On "God hearkened to her," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the prayer. Calvin and Gill note that "God hearkened to her" — Rachel had at last sought God by prayer (her early impatience, vv.1-2, giving way to prayer); her prayer and faith obtained what envy and the mandrakes could not; the answered prayer; the lesson that what is sought of God (not of man or means) is given in his time; the prayer heard.

On "opened her womb," the grammatical-historical note the gift: "opened her womb" — God gave Rachel conception (the barren made fruitful, as Sarah, Rebekah, and later Hannah); the gift of God alone (Psalm 127:3); the birth of Joseph (vv.23-24); the long-barren Rachel at last a mother; the providence in God's own time.
